::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In New Jersey, under the general rules of prison release for people convicted of nonviolent and some violent offenses, eligibility for parole release (with credit earnings) can be as early as 20 percent of the maximum term. For designated serious violent and sex offenses, parole eligibility cannot occur before serving 85 percent of the maximum term.¹
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility In 2019, 27 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 73 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2018, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 70 percent. We've projected that from 2019 to 2023, the percent of people past parole eligibility will increase by 13.8 percent. Note: 2019 data uses projections. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 62 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2019, 95 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age ```{=html}

Data is not available. New Jersey submitted incomplete or missing data to the National Corrections Reporting Program in 2019.

``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 39 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 61 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people in prison past parole eligibility were incarcerated for drug (29 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths In 2019, 35 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 and 4.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology ATTENTION, ERROR: This state should not be in the tool

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent for most people, and 85 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). Second, the absolute min. sentence of 9 months when the most serious offense is non-violent and for people with offenses of more than . years when the most serious offense is violent is computed. The previously computed rules-based estimate was replaced by the absolute min. sentence if the latter was longer. All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Because of this, data reported for New Jersey is from 2010, except for the 2023 estimate.

Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁴
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: New Jersey *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, June 2022), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-06/new_jersey_doi_report_6_29_22.pdf.](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-06/new_jersey_doi_report_6_29_22.pdf.)

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43. For offenses with special restrictions, see Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Report: New Jersey (Minneapolis, MN: Robina Institute, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-06/new_jersey_doi_report_6_29_22.pdf, 11–12. .

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.New Jersey Department of Corrections, Population Characteristics Report on January 1, 2024 (Trenton, NJ: Office of Compliance and Strategic Planning, New Jersey Department of Corrections, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://www.nj.gov/corrections/pdf/offender_statistics/2024/Entire%20Population%20Characteristics%20Report_Final%20Draft.pdf, 2. Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2019, the prison population decreased 26 percent, changing from 25,007 in 2010 to 18,613 in 2019. ```{=html}

``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2018, the number of people released from prison decreased 35 percent, dropping from 12,128 in 2010 to 7,847 in 2018. ```{=html}

## Notes on Disparities **Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes** In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex. The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility. **Metrics Displayed in Visualizations** Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex: - **RRI of Parole Release:** This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole. - **Average Time Past Parole Eligibility:** For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups. - **Average Total Time Served:** We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities. **Data Considerations** Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology. Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states: - Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations. - Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals. - Including 'Other' Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis. **Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation** Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis. ::: --- title: "New Jersey" ---
